findings.expected: Drop 'unsafe-inline'; pin scripts via per-request nonce or hash; add object-src 'none' and base-uri 'none'.
findings.rationale: Inline scripts allowed on a session-bearing dashboard means any reflected/stored XSS sink is directly exploitable.
A 1-day HSTS window is barely better than none — the first visit window is fully unprotected and includeSubDomains is missing.

findings.expected: session=...; Path=/; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax (or Strict); consider __Host- prefix
findings.rationale: Session cookie has no Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite attributes — XSS-readable and CSRF-friendly on an authenticated dashboard.
Pinning the nginx minor version helps an attacker target known CVEs without fingerprinting.
PHP 7.4 is end-of-life; advertising the exact version invites version-specific exploit attempts.
A dashboard with no need for these capabilities should disable them to reduce exposure from embedded third-party content.
Without an explicit policy, links out from the dashboard may leak full URLs containing identifiers.
